content:
3 min read
Just now

--

I once spent a week explaining a feature to a designer. She nodded. She smiled. She said “got it.”
A week later, the designs came back.
I opened the file expecting my baby. What I got was… someone else’s baby. Cute kid, wrong parents.
Different flow. Different screens. A whole new feature I definitely did not ask for, staring back at me like, “surprise!”
I checked the file name three times. Same project. Same me. Somehow, not my idea anymore.
My first instinct was to blame the designer. My second, better instinct the one that actually taught me something was to ask myself what I had actually handed her.
Turns out, not much. I’d given her a task list and quietly assumed the story would teleport into her brain along with it. It didn’t. And here’s the thing: that’s not on her. A designer can only build the product you actually described not the one that was living, fully formed, in your head. When the story has gaps, a good designer doesn’t stall out. She fills them in with her own smart, reasonable, completely different interpretation.
That’s not a failure on her end. That’s a gap on mine.
Lesson learned the expensive way: if you don’t explain the plot, don’t be surprised when someone else writes the ending ,and don’t blame them for writing a good one.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s the Trap
So there I was, staring at this stranger-feature wearing my project’s name tag, and the designer said, with total confidence: “This is basically what you described.”
And honestly? From where she was standing, it probably was. She’d taken what I gave her and run with it logically. The trap wasn’t her confidence — the trap was mine, sitting there ready to nod along just to avoid an awkward conversation.
Confidence is contagious. Say something with enough conviction and people start doubting their own memory. I almost said “yeah, I guess this works” just to keep the peace.
Don’t do that. Not because the other person is wrong to be confident — but because you’re the one holding the original vision, and it’s your job to check it against reality, calmly and clearly, before it moves any further down the line.
Say No — Professionally
So I said the harder, more useful sentence: “This isn’t quite it yet and that’s probably on me.”
Not “I don’t love it.” Not a vague, deflating “let’s tweak a few things.” A clear, calm, collaborative version of let’s get back on track:
“This isn’t aligned with what we discussed. I think I need to walk you through the flow and the goal again, more clearly this time. Let’s revisit it together.”
No drama. No blame. Just an honest acknowledgment that alignment is a two-way job and if the output missed the mark, the input probably needs another pass.
Follow Up, Every Time
Here’s what I know now that past-me, riding high after that first “aligned” meeting, did not: one good conversation is not a finish line. It’s a checkpoint. And it’s my job to keep checking — not to hand off an idea and hope it survives the journey on its own.
The loop looks like this:

• Explain clearly: the flow, the business goal, the non-negotiables. All three, every time.

• Review carefully: not skimming for “close enough,” but checking against what you actually meant.

• Push back when needed : kindly, but without apologizing for having a clear vision.

• Confirm before it moves forward : because a nod in a meeting is not the same as shared understanding.
Skip step 4, and you’re back in my inbox, staring at someone else’s baby wearing your project’s name.
Your product deserves to look like your vision and getting there isn’t about finding someone to blame when it doesn’t. It’s about making sure the people building it can actually see what you see, clearly enough that they don’t have to guess. So say it clearly. Check it closely. Own the gaps when they’re yours. And when it’s off track?
Say so, and help bring it back.
